title: Keeping Pekin bantams
subtitle: A small, sociable breed for gardens with limited space.
excerpt: Pekin bantams are a true bantam breed, there is no large counterpart. They are round-bodied, low-slung, and covered in feathering that reaches the feet. They lay small eggs through spring and summer, go broody readily, and cause less damage to a garden than a standard-sized layer.
sourceNotes: Poultry Club of Great Britain breed standard for Pekin bantam. DEFRA Code of Practice for the Welfare of Laying Hens, gov.uk. Wright, L., The Practical Poultry Keeper (1867), Internet Archive, for historical breed context.

glossaryTerms.definition: A breed that has no large-fowl equivalent. True bantams were originally domesticated small, rather than being bred down from a standard-sized bird.
Foot and lower-leg feathering found on breeds including Pekin, Cochin, and Brahma. Requires clean dry ground and regular checking for feather loss, scaly leg mite, and mud-ball build-up.

content.text: : small in their own right, not bred down from a large fowl. A standard Pekin cockerel weighs around 680 g; a hen around 570 g. Their low-to-the-ground build and thickly feathered bodies make them look larger than they are.

Pekins don't fly much, so a half-metre perimeter fence is usually enough to keep them within a garden section. The coop needs 0.5 square metres per bird inside, with perches no higher than 30 cm from the floor, their build makes higher perches harder to reach and the landing impact is harder on their joints.

The nesting box should have a low lip; the birds' belly feathering brushes the floor on entry. Clean, dry litter is particularly important for Pekins: wet bedding packs into their foot feathering and causes sores.

need weekly attention. After wet weather, mud and dung can build up into hard balls around the outer toes. Left for more than a few days these balls constrict the toe and cut off blood supply. Check each bird's feet once a week in autumn and winter.

To remove a mud ball: hold the foot over a bowl of warm water and soak for a minute, then ease the ball away from the toe with your fingers. Don't pull feathers; work the mud free. If the toe underneath looks pale or swollen, the circulation has been restricted and a vet visit is appropriate.

Scaly leg mite is easier to pick up in feathered-footed breeds because the mite lives under the foot scales and the feathering conceals early signs. Check the scales on the lower leg regularly; raised, crusty scales that were previously smooth indicate mite. The

Expect 80 to 120 eggs per hen per year in a good spring and summer. The eggs are small, roughly 35 to 40 g, and pale cream or tinted. Production drops noticeably from September and stops in winter without additional lighting.

Pekins are strongly broody. A hen going broody in June and sitting for three weeks before the eggs fail (or hatching successfully) will then be out of lay for another four to six weeks. A four-hen flock with two broodys in summer produces very few eggs from June to August. If you want consistent laying,
early is worth doing, or accept that Pekins are a seasonal-eggs-and-occasional-chicks breed rather than a reliable year-round layer.

Pekins handle cold well but not wet. In persistent rain, their foot feathering gets waterlogged and they become reluctant to move around. Cover a section of their run to give them dry ground to stand on, or confine them to the coop on the worst days and let them out in the afternoon when the ground has drained.
